1.19. We recommended and Management agreed to:
1.19.a. Obtain and submit to the Audit Team the acknowledgement receipts
from the payees who did not affix their signatures in the payrolls and
from those that were received by persons other than the claimant that
they actually received the amounts due them;
1.19.b. Demand the immediate liquidation of cash advances which purpose/s
have been served/completed;
1.19.c. Refund the unclaimed payments for payrolls on Aid to Individuals in Crisis
Situations (AICS), and honoraria or incentives for barangay officials and staff
after a reasonable period to avoid the accumulation of unclaimed funds for
around two (2) to three (3) months in the hands of the AO and delay the
liquidation period;

their signature on the payroll upon receipt, and require authority and
identification from the claimant, if claimed by person other than the
payee; and
1.19.e. Strictly adopt the “no liquidation, no cash advance” policy by
enforcing the submission of the Municipal Accountant’s Certification that
previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the
books as a documentary requirement before a new cash advance is
granted.

avoid overpayments.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant, General Services Officer-Designate, and Municipal Engineer comply with the duties and responsibilities enumerated in the COA Circular No. 2015-008, as follows:

V. Accounting and Reporting Procedures:

“1.  Conduct an inventory of the local roads under the LGU’s jurisdiction. (GSO with Inventory Committee)

“2.  Reconcile the list of inventory per local road inventory report and road map with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. (GSO and Municipal Accountant)

“3.  Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads with identified cost. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“4.  Determine the components of local roads identified in the inventory without corresponding cost and recognize the cost of each component at its fair value. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“5.  Depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and shall serve as the basis for recording in the general ledger. (Municipal Accountant)

“7.  Compute separately the depreciation for the road components taking into consideration the following factors:
a. Initial cost, and
b. Useful life
(Municipal Accountant)

“8.  Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: a) road lot, b) road pavement, c) drainage and slope protection structures and d) other miscellaneous structures. (Municipal Accountant)

“9. Provide complete description of the road and its components in the subsidiary ledger and reconcile with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure completeness. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“10.  Assess the local road at every reporting date, whether there is any indication that a component of the local road may be impaired.” (Municipal Accountant)

VI.  Reporting Guidelines

“1.  The General Services Officer shall at the end of the accounting period render a Report on Local Road Network of the local government unit concerned (Annex A). (GSO)

“2.  The total road network system shall be disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the Agency (Annex B). (Municipal Accountant)

“3.  The Inventory Committee shall prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System of the local government unit (Annex C). (Inventory Committee with GSO).

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant, General Services Officer-Designate, and Municipal Engineer comply with the duties and responsibilities enumerated in the COA Circular No. 2015-008, as follows:

V. Accounting and Reporting Procedures:

“1.  Conduct an inventory of the local roads under the LGU’s jurisdiction. (GSO with Inventory Committee)

“2.  Reconcile the list of inventory per local road inventory report and road map with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. (GSO and Municipal Accountant)

“3.  Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads with identified cost. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“4.  Determine the components of local roads identified in the inventory without corresponding cost and recognize the cost of each component at its fair value. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“5.  Depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and shall serve as the basis for recording in the general ledger. (Municipal Accountant)

“7.  Compute separately the depreciation for the road components taking into consideration the following factors:
 Initial cost, and
Useful life
(Municipal Accountant)

“8.  Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: a) road lot, b) road pavement, c) drainage and slope protection structures and d) other miscellaneous structures. (Municipal Accountant)

“9.  Provide complete description of the road and its components in the subsidiary ledger and reconcile with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure completeness. (Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer)

“10.  Assess the local road at every reporting date, whether there is any indication that a component of the local road may be impaired.” (Municipal Accountant)

VI.  Reporting Guidelines

“1.  The General Services Officer shall at the end of the accounting period render a Report on Local Road Network of the local government unit concerned (Annex A). (GSO)

“2.  The total road network system shall be disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the Agency (Annex B). (Municipal Accountant)

“3.  The Inventory Committee shall prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System of the local government unit (Annex C). (Inventory Committee with GSO)”

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
submit the afore-cited CY 2017 vouchers soonest;
henceforth, submit the required reports and documents within the prescribed timelines, as follows:
Municipal Accountant:
Annual Financial Statements with supporting journals and ledgers – not later than February 14 of the following year:

DVs/Payrolls/LVs/Receipts - not later than the 10th day of the ensuing month;

Municipal Treasurer:

CRAAF – not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month.
Municipal Engineer:
Consolidated Quarterly Report of Government Projects/Programs/ Activities not later than the 20th day after the end of the quarter
LDRRMO:
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF not later than the 15th day of the ensuing month.
implement adequate measures that will enable the Municipality to meet such deadlines; and
consider enforcing sanctions to erring officers and employees.
Further, Management should contemplate on filling up the Administrative Assistant IV position at the accounting office at the soonest possible time to augment the existing permanent personnel.

We have recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile their cash balances periodically, by tracing the causes of the discrepancies and effecting the necessary adjustments to ensure completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions and the balance of cash in bank;
the Municipal Accountant record the balances of the three TF bank accounts, and henceforth, prepare BRS for all accounts maintained by the Municipality with its depository banks on a “one bank account: one BRS” ratio; and
Management to comply with the DOF Circulars by transferring soonest its two (2) accounts from PNB - Lagawe Branch to LBP - Lagawe Branch or other AGDBs.

We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to capitalize the borrowing costs relative to the construction of the Trade Center Building II to comply with PPSAS requirements, and to present fairly the balances of affected accounts.  Thereafter, disclose in the Notes to FS the accounting policy adapted and other necessary details on borrowing costs.

We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2015-009 or the Revised Chart of Accounts and make the necessary adjustments in the GF and TF books to present appropriately the counterpart funds in the ensuing FS; and
henceforth, record the transfer and receipt of LGU counterpart funds as Transfer for Project Equity Share/LGU Counterpart and Transfer from GF of LGU Counterpart accounts, respectively, and present them in the Statement of Financial Performance under the appropriate captions.

We have recommended and Management agreed to, henceforth, require the different end-user offices to prepare PPMPs, instead of APPs, in the prescribed format, where all  procurement requirements for the year are incorporated,  submit the same to the Budget Office, then to the BAC Secretariat for evaluation and consolidation into a comprehensive, workable and realistic APP for approval by the HOPE as basis for procurement.

We  have recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of the Government Procurement Law and its Implementing Rules and Regulations by:
strengthening the procurement planning and execution of planned activities and programs to ensure timely conduct of the required procurement process ahead of the actual activity, including the consolidation of the goods to be procured, then adopting competitive bidding as the rule of thumb.  Thereafter, whenever there is need to replenish goods, resort to Repeat Order procurement, subject to conditions provided in Sec. 51 of the IRR. Other alternative modes of procurement shall be resorted to only whenever justified by the conditions and within the threshold provided therein;
complying with the required number of three price quotations whenever Shopping is resorted to; and
checking the authenticity and completeness of procurement supporting documents,  particularly the identities of persons signing on behalf of the suppliers, to establish verifiability and liability of the persons transacting with the Municipality.
We have recommended that Management require all persons liable to immediately settle their suspensions, disallowances and charges.

We have reiterated our recommendation in prior years’ audit reports, that Management, specifically the Municipal Accountant, to record the following adjusting entries to transfer the unspent MF-MOOE and QRF to the TF books, in accordance with the above guidelines.  Henceforth, to record LDRRMF expenditures/transactions using the herein enumerated journal entries, to ensure their correct presentation in the ensuing FS and to establish proper accountability on disaster funds:
Table 17.  Pro-forma Accounting Entries for the Transfer of Unspent MOOE and QRF

We have recommended and Management agreed to cause the reversion of the unspent balance of the LDRRMF/Calamity Fund, totaling P140,702.11, to the unappropriated surplus of the GF and thereafter, appropriate it for other social services through legislative enactment.

We have recommended and Management agreed to cause the prompt and complete remittance of all moneys collected and withheld from employees and supplier/contractors to BIR and Philhealth to avoid penalties and surcharges, among others.

We have recommended that Management fast track the implementation of the BUB/ADM projects in accordance with the timelines provided for in the MOA, and collect the LDs from the defaulting contractors mentioned in sub-paragraph 15.24.3.

We have reiterated that management:
compute for the liquidated damages of the above projects, then collect from the contractors, either through deduction from the retention fees or any amount due them, or require cash payments hereof; and
henceforth, to provide complete data in the supporting documents, i.e. contract duration and percentage of accomplishment on the date of contract expiry.

We have recommended that management:
limit the hiring of Job Order personnel only for approved local projects authorized by the Sangguniang Bayan, and for a period not exceeding six months, pursuant to Section 77 of RA 7160;
hire personnel through casual appointments pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 40, series of 1998, for essential and necessary services; and
evaluate the existing plantilla of personnel to determine if there is a need for increase in manpower to justify the creation of additional positions, subject, however, to personnel service limitation.

b. henceforth, record the transfer and receipt of LGU counterpart funds as
Transfer for Project Equity Share/LGU Counterpart and Transfer from GF
of LGU Counterpart accounts, respectively, and present them in the
Statement of Financial Performance under the appropriate captions.
C. Special account in the General Fund not set-up for the 20% DF
13.19 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. set-up/maintain a special account in the GF for the 20% DF;
b. provide a separate set of subsidiary ledgers for the special account;
c. prepare the corresponding financial statements to form part of the schedules
of the GF trial balance; and
d. coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer and Sangguniang Bayan for the
opening of a separate depository account for this special fund.

We have recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan consider the afore-stated
recommendations and legislate amendments/repeals of the provisions of the
Revised MRC to suit pertinent provisions of the LGC.

We have recommended that management:
a) through the Municipal Accountant, implement strictly the guidelines
provided under LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013, on the grant of
Representation and Transportation Allowances to all LGU officials
entitled thereto;
b) collect the overpaid RATA;
c) through the Vice-Mayor as the presiding chair of the SB, instigate the
adoption/inclusion of rules in the IRP on the work schedules/performance
standards of all members, which shall be used in the determination of
percentage of RATA to be granted hereafter, in accordance with Item 7.5
of the above LBC; and
d) observe the rules on leave of absence.

We have recommended that management:
a. limit payroll deductions and follow the order of preference provided in the
GAA to ensure that all employees’ take home pay shall not be less than the
minimum allowed by law;
b. cease from being a withholding agent for loan obligations of employees in
institutions not allowed in the provisions above; and
c. hold the withholding agents/officials responsible for allowing the net take
home pay of the employees lesser than that allowed by law.

We have recommended that management, henceforth, increase the tax rate
from 2% to 3% for non-professionals, then withhold and remit the same to
BIR, in accordance with pertinent revenue rules and regulations.

We have recommended that management book up ND No. 2010-001-
100(2009), in as much as it has already become final and executory, and
continue to enforce settlement of the afore-cited disallowances.

We have recommended that management:
a. follow-up the approval of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan
from the NSWMC, and thereafter, request for funding of relevant projects
from the National SWM Fund in accordance with their guidelines;
b. continue to assist the remaining 10 barangays in the establishment of their
MRF;
c. continue to enhance awareness of constituents on SWM and develop
stricter and more effective implementation techniques of SWM guidelines
and ordinances; and
d. through the Sanggunian Bayan, expedite the passing of pending ordinances
relative to SWM that will ensure the protection of public health and
environment.

We have recommended that management, henceforth:
a. require the GAD Focal Point to administer the HGDG test on the LGU’s
GAD PPAs in the preparation of Accomplishment Report, as basis for
determining the actual expenditure that can be attributed to the GAD
Budget;
b. administer the HGDG test on its PPAs for CY 2017, the results of which
shall be the basis in estimating the cost of gender mainstreaming and GAD
Budget;
c. if and when necessary, conduct a training program on the HGDG for the
incoming year and seek assistance of a gender analysis expert in the use of
and/administration of the HGDG; and
d. expedite the implementation of GAD PPAs to accelerate gender
mainstreaming in the MLGU operations.

We have recommended that management, henceforth, require all elected
officials and heads of offices to undergo capacity-building programs on
GAD. For economy, invite competent speakers and conduct in-house training
to maximize the participation of the focal point system, elected officials,
heads of offices, employees and other stakeholders, i.e. Barangay officials, to
harmonize GAD-related activities in the ensuing year. Such capacity
building programs on GAD shall include, but not limited to: gender
sensitivity training, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender
analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming
Evaluation Framework, Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba Self-Assessment
Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines and others.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

direct the Property Custodian-designate to conduct physical count of PPE items, expedite the preparation of RPCPPE, and thereafter reconcile records with the Municipal Accountant; 

instruct the Accountant to analyze the 2014 PPE audit recommendations on the valuation and classification of PPE as against the present guidelines of the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS), and  effect appropriate adjusting entries to reflect their correct balances and classification in the FS in compliance with the latter; and


henceforth, instruct the Property Custodian-designate to submit promptly the RPCPPE to COA.
